+ PRINCETON UNIVERSITY [3 News That Matters]

+ PRINCETON'S KAMERON MERCER RECEIVES OFFER FROM CINCINNATI BEARCATS

Princeton High School's five-star basketball prospect Kameron Mercer has officially received a scholarship offer from the University of Cincinnati Bearcats. On 6th May 2026, Mercer announced the offer on his social media accounts, marking a significant milestone in his burgeoning athletic career.Mercer played a pivotal role in leading his Princeton High School team to victory in the Ohio Division I state basketball title as a sophomore. The 6-foot-5 shooting guard showcased his potential throughout the season, culminating in a 25-3 record for the Vikings. He averaged 14.2 points, 5.4 rebounds, and 3.4 assists per game, with notable performances including a season-high of 27 points against Colerain in January.The Cincinnati Bearcats are not alone in their pursuit of Mercer; they join an impressive list of programs including Florida State, Maryland, and Ohio State that have shown interest in the talented guard. Originally offered by the Bearcats in October 2024 while he was at Huntington Expressions Prep in West Virginia, Mercer’s recent visit to Cincinnati led to a renewed offer under new head coach Jerrod Calhoun.Mercer's impressive stats from the past season include being third in the Greater Miami Conference for assists and second in steals. His ability to perform against older competition at Huntington, averaging 11 points, six rebounds, and five assists, has only further elevated his profile among college recruiters. Additionally, he achieved two double-doubles during the season, demonstrating his all-around game.Accompanying Mercer on his visit to the Cincinnati Bearcats was his older brother, Jaylen Mercer, who is also garnering attention from various college programs as a promising edge rusher. Jaylen, a 6-foot-5, 260-pound athlete, has received offers from multiple schools, including the Bearcats, as he prepares for his graduation in 2027. This family connection is likely to play a role in Kameron's decision as he weighs the opportunities presented by the Bearcats and other schools.

+ JAMIE DING'S CELEBRITY STATUS AT PRINCETON UNIVERSITY BREAKFAST SPOT

Jamie Ding, the recent champion of 'Jeopardy!', has been making headlines since his impressive 31-day winning streak concluded. On the 5th of May, 2026, he was spotted at various locations in New Jersey, including popular breakfast spots near Princeton University.The Princeton University graduate finished his remarkable run on April 27, 2026, securing a place among the top five 'Jeopardy!' champions of all time. This impressive feat has placed him in the company of legendary contestants like Ken Jennings and Amy Schneider, with winnings totaling approximately $882,605.Among the locations where Ding was seen is PJ's Pancake House, a renowned establishment in Princeton, NJ. The owner, Marty Tuchman, took to social media to express his excitement at meeting Ding, highlighting the local pride that comes with the champion's recognition. PJ's Pancake House, situated at 154 Nassau St., is known for its welcoming atmosphere and delicious breakfast offerings, making it a fitting backdrop for such a celebrity sighting.In addition to PJ's, Jamie Ding was also recently spotted at Eat Gud Bakery in Hamilton, further emphasizing his connection to the Garden State. As a law student at Seton Hall, Ding continues to balance his academic pursuits with the newfound fame that comes from his 'Jeopardy!' success.As Jamie Ding's journey unfolds, the spotlight on Princeton University and its alumni continues to shine brightly. His incredible achievements on 'Jeopardy!' not only contribute to his personal narrative but also enhance the reputation of the institution that shaped him, leaving fans and investors alike curious about what he will do next.

+ PRINCETON'S YIYUN LI SECURES PULITZER PRIZE FOR MEMOIR

Princeton University professor Yiyun Li has been awarded the 2026 Pulitzer Prize for memoir for her impactful book 'Things in Nature Merely Grow.' The announcement came as a pleasant surprise to Li, who received a flurry of congratulatory messages on the afternoon of 5th May 2026.The Pulitzer Prize for memoir honors distinguished factual accounts by American authors. Li's book is described as 'a writer’s deeply moving and revelatory account of losing her younger son to suicide a little more than six years after her older son died in the same manner.' It has been praised for its austere yet defiant approach to acceptance, focusing on the power of language and the persistence of life.Li expressed her gratitude for the recognition, noting that the memoir was crafted during a challenging period in her life. She reflected, 'The book was written because I hold my faith in language, in thinking, and in carrying on, despite the difficulties.' The Pulitzer Prize, she added, confirms her belief in the transformative power of words.'Things in Nature Merely Grow' has also garnered other accolades, including the Andrew Carnegie Medal for Excellence in Nonfiction, and was a finalist for the National Book Award for nonfiction. The book has been featured in several notable lists, including The New York Times 100 Notable Books of 2025 and The New Yorker Best Books of 2025, highlighting its widespread acclaim.Judith Hamera, chair of the Lewis Center for the Arts at Princeton, commended Li's work, stating, 'This book is an especially powerful example of her extraordinary work in multiple genres.' Li, who joined Princeton's faculty in 2017, has authored 12 books and received numerous prestigious awards, further establishing her as a significant figure in contemporary literature.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ DUKE UNIVERSITY [3 News That Matters]

+ DUKE UNIVERSITY TO HONOR NEARLY 7,000 GRADUATES AT THIS YEAR'S COMMENCEMENT

Duke University will award nearly 7,000 undergraduate, graduate, and professional degrees during its annual commencement ceremony on 10th May 2026. The ceremony is scheduled to begin at 9 a.m. at Wallace Wade Stadium, with Duke President Vincent E. Price presiding over the event. Notably, the commencement address will be delivered by alumna Amy Hood, who is the Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer of Microsoft.The undergraduate cohort will see approximately 1,785 students receiving their degrees, including around 300 from North Carolina. This year's graduates represent the first group to complete Duke's QuadEx experience, a living and learning model designed to enhance both the residential and academic experiences while fostering a stronger sense of community among students. First-year students, who began their journey in fall 2022, were placed in East Campus residence halls and subsequently moved to West Campus as a cohesive group after their first year.While the commencement ceremony serves as the signature event of graduation weekend, additional celebrations will take place later on Sunday and into Monday, focusing on individual schools, programs, and campus groups. Duke will also stream the event live on the university's YouTube channel, providing an opportunity for a wider audience to participate in the occasion. Social media platforms will be utilized to keep the community engaged and informed.Two students will deliver welcome remarks during the ceremony. Juliet L. Pe aranda P rez, a first-generation college student from Puerto Rico, is set to represent the undergraduate class. She has a background shaped by significant life events, including the impact of Hurricane Mar a, which has influenced her understanding of resilience and community. Lucas Bellaiche, a Ph.D. candidate in psychology and neuroscience from Arkansas, will speak on behalf of the graduate students. His research integrates arts and sciences, focusing on the positive effects of creative thinking on mental health.Duke University will also honor four individuals with honorary degrees during the ceremony, including the commencement speaker Amy Hood. Other honorees include Abby Wambach, the retired soccer star and two-time Olympic gold medalist; Dianne R. McIntyre, a distinguished dancer and choreographer; and Cass R. Sunstein, a prominent legal scholar. As part of the commencement festivities, ample general parking will be available near Wallace Wade Stadium, with specific arrangements for guests with mobility impairments.

+ DUKE UNIVERSITY WELCOMES NROTC SCHOLAR FROM SEATTLE

SEATTLE (April 28, 2026) — Moira Kinney, a student at Holy Names Academy and prospective Duke University student, has been awarded a Naval Reserve Officers Training Corps (NROTC) scholarship by the Navy Talent Acquisition Group Pacific Northwest. This prestigious scholarship, announced on 5th May 2026, offers selected students full tuition along with certain educational fees or room and board at participating colleges and universities, providing additional financial benefits while preparing them for a commission as Navy or Marine Corps officers.The NROTC scholarship is highly competitive, designed to attract high-quality candidates who will contribute to the readiness of America’s Navy. Duke University, known for its rigorous academic programs and commitment to leadership development, stands to benefit from Kinney’s selection. The Navy Talent Acquisition Group Pacific Northwest oversees more than 34 enlisted and officer recruiting stations across Washington, Idaho, Montana, and Alaska, focusing on inspiring future leaders within the military.Kinney's achievement not only highlights her academic prowess but also reflects her commitment to serve and lead in the armed forces. By choosing Duke University, she joins a vibrant community of scholars and leaders, furthering her education in an environment that encourages excellence. The NROTC program at Duke is recognized for its strong emphasis on leadership training and military education, making it an ideal choice for Kinney as she prepares for her future role as an officer.The financial support provided by the NROTC scholarship will significantly alleviate the burden of tuition costs and enable Kinney to focus on her studies and military training. This scholarship is a testament to the Navy's dedication to nurturing the next generation of military leaders, ensuring that they receive a quality education while fostering their leadership capabilities.As Kinney embarks on this new chapter at Duke University, her journey serves as an inspiration to other students considering a path in military service. The combination of academic excellence and military training provided by the NROTC program positions her to make meaningful contributions both at Duke University and in her future career as a Navy or Marine Corps officer.

+ DUKE UNIVERSITY RESTORES STUDENTS FOR JUSTICE IN PALESTINE CHAPTER AMID CONTROVERSY

Duke University has reinstated its Students for Justice in Palestine (SJP) chapter after a brief suspension related to an antisemitic social media post. The decision comes after an investigation by the university's Office of Institutional Equity determined that a graphic shared on March 13, 2026, did not breach university policies concerning discrimination and harassment.The contested post featured a 1970 graphic from the Black Panther Party's newspaper, illustrating two pigs — one labeled 'U.S. imperialism' and the other labeled 'Zionism.' Although the imagery was deemed offensive for its antisemitic implications, Sharon Gooding, associate vice president for institutional equity, stated that there was insufficient evidence to suggest a hostile educational environment had been fostered as a result of the post.Following this assessment, Duke University’s Student Affairs department lifted the suspension and the accompanying funding freeze. However, the university mandated that the post be permanently removed from all platforms. The decision has sparked discussions among students and faculty regarding the balance between free speech and the university's responsibility to maintain an inclusive environment.Jessie Appleby, an attorney for the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression, expressed satisfaction with the reinstatement of SJP but emphasized that the group should not have faced suspension over the post. Appleby noted that the investigation into SJP could deter students from voicing unpopular opinions, as it highlighted the university's willingness to act against controversial speech.In a response to the suspension, SJP argued that it represented a broader issue of silencing dissenting voices within the student body. Ben Adams, senior associate dean of students for QuadEX at Duke University, clarified that the restrictions placed on SJP were standard practice while a complaint review was underway. The reinstatement of SJP signals a complex interplay between free expression and institutional policy at Duke University.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ HARVARD UNIVERSITY [3 News That Matters]

+ HARVARD UNIVERSITY'S TIAHNA PADILLA MAKES HISTORY WITH MA SORENSEN AWARD WIN

CAMBRIDGE, Mass.– Harvard University women’s rugby senior Tiahna Padilla has won the prestigious MA Sorensen Award, given each year to the top collegiate woman rugby player in the USA, becoming the first student-athlete in program history to win the award. The date of this announcement is 6th May 2026, marking a significant milestone not only for Padilla but also for Harvard University and its rugby program.The MA Sorensen Award honors MaryAnn (MA) Sorensen, a trailblazing player, coach, and physician who captained the United States to a championship at the 1991 Women’s Rugby World Cup. Sorensen's legacy celebrates excellence both on the field and beyond, and she continues to present the award annually at the Washington Athletic Club awards dinner in Seattle. Padilla's achievement is a testament to the growing prominence of Harvard University in women’s collegiate rugby.Padilla has made history as she earns collegiate women’s rugby’s top honor after being a four-time finalist and a four-time nominee for the award. As the senior captain of the team, she has been instrumental in leading Harvard University to a historic three-peat, earning MVP honors for the second time this fall and First-Team All-NIRA recognition for the fourth consecutive year. Her consistent performance has solidified her place among the elite athletes in the sport.A member of the USA senior pipeline since her first year, Padilla holds the program record for career assists. This season alone, she tallied impressive statistics including 95 tackles, 11 turnovers, six assists, and 66 carries, while also setting up two clutch tries. Her game-winning play in the Dartmouth Ivy Title match, characterized by a last-second line break and offload, exemplifies her skill and leadership on the field.Tiahna Padilla will be formally recognized for her remarkable achievements on June 13th, 2026, during a gala at the Washington Athletic Club in Seattle. This event will not only celebrate her individual accomplishments but also highlight the growing success and recognition of Harvard University's women’s rugby program, further elevating its status in collegiate athletics.

+ HARVARD UNIVERSITY AND LINUX FOUNDATION UNVEIL KEYRING WALLET FOR DATA PRIVACY

In a significant development in the realm of digital privacy, Harvard University has launched an open-source identity verification tool called the Keyring wallet. This initiative, developed by the Applied Social Media Lab (ASML) at the Berkman Klein Center for Internet & Society, aims to enhance user privacy while facilitating identity verification. The wallet was officially unveiled in April 2026 and aligns with a growing trend towards identity verification and age assurance in the digital landscape.The Keyring wallet allows users to authenticate their identity through biometric methods while retaining control over the data they choose to share with online platforms. This includes the capacity to link social media profiles to verify account ownership, a feature demonstrated last year using a browser extension called CredSnap. Brendan Miller, principal engineer at ASML, highlighted that this could be particularly advantageous for content creators and publishers who want to establish their identity across different platforms.Another notable feature of the Keyring wallet is its ability to share age-related information while maintaining user privacy. By leveraging mobile driver’s licenses (mDLs) issued by the Department of Motor Vehicles, the wallet can confirm a user's age without revealing unnecessary personal data. This capability addresses the increasing demand for age verification on social media platforms, providing a solution that respects user privacy.Additionally, the Keyring wallet introduces a novel concept of person-to-person relationship credentials. This feature enables individuals to verify their connections with one another without involving intermediaries such as LinkedIn. For instance, two professionals meeting at a conference could confirm their interaction, thereby building a trusted social network. This decentralized approach has the potential to create a trust graph that could serve various purposes, including proof of personhood and content provenance.Despite its promising features, the Keyring wallet faces challenges in securing adoption from institutions and corporations that currently benefit from controlling user data. Yajaira Gonzalez, a product leader at ASML, emphasized that the existing incentives for these entities are often misaligned with the model proposed by the Keyring wallet. However, growing user demand for data control could prompt a shift in this dynamic, making the open-source app available on GitHub a notable development in the identity verification landscape.

+ UCHICAGO'S CHEMISTS BREAK NEW GROUND IN LIGHT-MATTER HYBRID IMAGING

UChicago chemists have unveiled a groundbreaking method to visualize light-matter hybrid particles known as polaritons, which could pave the way for advancements in future computing and electronic technologies. On the 1st of May, 2026, the team published their findings in Nature Communications, showcasing an ultrafast imaging technique that captures the motion of these quasiparticles traveling through a specially engineered crystal.To achieve this, the researchers developed a sophisticated imaging technique that combines the ease of laser use with the high-resolution capabilities of electron microscopy. This innovative 'camera' enabled them to observe polaritons, which are hybrid particles formed from both light and matter, as they moved across a flake of molybdenum oxydichloride (MoOCl2)—a layered crystalline material. The study demonstrated that these quasiparticles could travel significant distances without losing energy, a notable achievement in the field of photonics.The ability to manipulate polaritons is critical for the development of advanced technologies that rely on guiding light at extremely small scales. As Sarah King, assistant professor of chemistry at UChicago, explained, controlling light is inherently challenging due to its tendency to spread out and fade. However, by fusing light with matter to form polaritons, the researchers found a way to steer these particles more effectively. The unique properties of MoOCl2, which behaves differently depending on the direction of light, further enhance the potential for controlling light within photonic technologies.The research team, led by King and postdoctoral scholar Atreyie Ghosh, utilized a technique called time-resolved photoemission electron microscopy. This method allowed them to capture a 'molecular movie' of polaritons in motion, revealing their long-range propagation capabilities. The findings indicate that MoOCl2 could serve as a promising platform for next-generation optoelectronic devices, including computer chips that leverage light instead of electricity.As the UChicago researchers continue to explore this novel material, they aim to uncover fundamental questions regarding the interaction of light with the crystal's atomic structure. Their ultimate goal is to develop new methods for controlling light, which could significantly impact various fields, including quantum information science. With this innovative approach, UChicago is poised to contribute to the evolution of optical technology, offering exciting possibilities for future applications.

+ COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY UNVEILS NEW SOLAR PANEL FORECASTING METHOD

Researchers at Columbia University have developed a cutting-edge computer-vision technique that forecasts a solar panel's annual energy production in dense urban environments. This announcement, made on the 21st of April 2026, details how the new system utilizes a single 360deg image to assess various factors affecting solar energy output.The innovative method relies on a hemispherical photograph to infer essential parameters such as sky visibility, scene geometry, and illumination. According to reports, the system employs a neural network that has been trained on synthetic images and subsequently fine-tuned using real urban data. This allows it to simulate shadows and reflections, ultimately producing an accurate irradiance forecast for solar panels, as mentioned in recent coverage.Field tests conducted in upper Manhattan, particularly at bikeshare docking stations, demonstrated the effectiveness of this new approach. The research indicates that by reorienting certain panels based on the analysis, annual energy yield could increase by up to 30%. This significant improvement highlights the potential for Columbia University's technology to optimize solar energy capture in urban settings.The forecasting technique extracts visual cues from high-dynamic-range images, analyzing shadows, textures, and structural lines to infer critical scene parameters. This sophisticated process not only estimates sun directions but also simulates direct and diffuse irradiance, integrating energy throughput over various solar trajectories. The tool, along with a dedicated capture device named Solaris, has been designed specifically for urban applications.While traditional methods often rely on 3D city models and ray-tracing simulations, the Columbia University team argues that these models frequently overlook smaller features that can significantly impact shading patterns. As urban areas continue to grow, this innovative approach may offer a more accurate and efficient means of forecasting solar energy potential, paving the way for enhanced site-level solar analytics and practical applications across various urban landscapes.

+ UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA'S HOCKEY LEGACY: A LOOK BACK AND AHEAD

As of 5th May 2026, the landscape of NCAA Division I hockey has shifted, yet the University of Pennsylvania remains a notable case study in the viability of collegiate hockey programs.The university's hockey program was once a proud Division I Independent, established in 1965, and became a member of the Eastern College Athletic Conference (ECAC) shortly thereafter. During its early years, the program enjoyed significant support, highlighted by alumni from the Class of 1923 who funded the construction of the Class of 1923 Arena, a facility that still stands today as a potential venue for NCAA hockey.Despite this promising start, the program faced an abrupt termination in 1978, leading to widespread discontent among students and alumni. + STANFORD UNIVERSITY UNCOVERS DISTINCT GENETIC LINEAGE OF 'GHOST ELEPHANTS'

In a groundbreaking discovery, researchers at Stanford University have revealed that a population of elephants in Angola's high-altitude wetlands possesses a unique genetic lineage. This finding was made possible through the extraction of DNA from dung, leading to the identification of these so-called 'ghost elephants'. The study was formally documented on 6th May 2026, and it has drawn considerable attention from the conservation community and the general public alike.Conservation biologist Steve Boyes has long sought to uncover the truth behind these elusive creatures, which are believed to be nocturnal giants residing in a remote part of Angola. After capturing their image with a motion-sensor camera in 2024, Boyes partnered with scientists from Stanford University to delve deeper into the genetic origins of these elephants. The results were unexpected; the ghost elephants are genetically distinct from any previously sequenced populations and share a closer resemblance to elephants found in Namibia, located hundreds of miles to the south.Led by Dmitri Petrov, the Michelle and Kevin Douglas Professor at Stanford, the genomic analysis utilized advanced techniques to decode the genetic material found in fecal samples. Petrov emphasized the growing capabilities of DNA sequencing technologies. 'DNA is the molecule of life, and people have figured out how to read it faster and faster,' he stated, highlighting the potential of such research to inform conservation efforts.The quest to understand the ghost elephants is part of a broader narrative captured in a National Geographic documentary directed by Werner Herzog. The film chronicles Boyes' journey through the rugged terrain of Angola, showcasing the challenges faced by the research team. The collaboration between Boyes and Stanford scientists, including senior scientist Katie Solari, has not only provided insights into the genetics of these elephants but also raised new questions about their evolutionary history and ecological significance.As the research progresses, Stanford University scientists hope to uncover more about the ghost elephants' population dynamics and their relationship to other elephant populations. The study has already yielded crucial information about individual identification and genetic diversity, underscoring the importance of non-invasive methods in wildlife research. With ongoing investigations, the story of Angola's ghost elephants continues to unfold, offering a compelling glimpse into the complexities of conservation biology.

+ STANFORD UNIVERSITY STUDY REVEALS AI FEEDBACK BIAS FAVORING BLACK AND FEMALE STUDENTS

A recent study from Stanford University has found that artificial intelligence grading systems may provide more positive feedback to black and female students while offering more criticism to white students. On 6th May 2026, the study titled "Marked Pedagogies: Examining Linguistic Biases in Personalized Automated Writing Feedback" analyzed approximately 600 essays written by middle school students, focusing on how AI-generated feedback varied based on demographic descriptors assigned to each student.Researchers submitted each essay to an AI model 13 times, altering descriptors such as race, gender, motivation level, and learning ability. The findings revealed what researchers termed "positive feedback bias" and "feedback withholding bias," indicating a concerning disparity in the treatment of students based on their identities. The study highlighted that students identified as Black, Hispanic, Asian, female, unmotivated, and learning-disabled received less constructive criticism and more praise compared to their counterparts, while male, White, motivated, and high-achieving students faced more distant feedback.The feedback provided to black students was often identity-based, with responses such as, "Consider how community service can specifically empower you and your peers as young Black leaders." In contrast, feedback aimed at white students was more focused on content, including directions like, "Try to avoid informal language and first-person perspective in an argumentative essay." Such differences in feedback raise critical questions about the effectiveness of AI in educational settings.Mei Tan, the lead author of the study, expressed concerns regarding the implications of these findings. In an interview, Tan stated that the AI models appear to be reflecting the biases inherent in human behavior. "Maybe a takeaway is that we shouldn’t leave the pedagogy to the large language model," Tan emphasized, suggesting that human oversight remains essential in educational contexts where AI is increasingly utilized.As schools continue to adopt automated tools for grading and instruction, the results of the Stanford University study underscore the need for careful consideration of how AI systems are designed and implemented. The findings prompt a broader discussion about the role of artificial intelligence in education and the potential risks of perpetuating existing biases through automated feedback mechanisms.

ACTIVITIES:

Crowley Maritime Corporation, founded in 1892, is a family-owned logistics and maritime services company headquartered in Jacksonville, Florida. Employing over 5,300 people, it operates in shipping, energy, and supply chain management, generating over $2 billion annually. Crowley provides ocean transport, LNG distribution, and logistics for government and commercial clients. In 2024, it expanded renewable energy logistics, supporting offshore wind projects. Its culture emphasizes safety, innovation, and sustainability, with robust employee training. Competing with Maersk, Crowley stands out for its U.S.-focused operations and government contracts. Recent initiatives include decarbonization efforts and digital tracking systems, aligning with green logistics trends.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME [3 News That Matters]

+ CROWLEY MARITIME SECURES PERMIT TO STUDY FUEL TERMINAL AT PORT MCKENZIE

Crowley Maritime has secured permission to study the development of a large fuel terminal at Port McKenzie, a move that could broaden the company's footprint in Alaska's Matanuska-Susitna Borough. The permit, granted for a comprehensive feasibility study, was announced on January 7, 2026, as part of Crowley's plan to explore a fuel-terminal complex serving both marine and land operations.If realized, the terminal would bolster the local fuel supply chain and support regional industries amid rising demand. By leveraging Port McKenzie's strategic location, Crowley aims to streamline operations and attract other businesses to the area, potentially marking a milestone for future investment and growth in the borough. Crowley now proceeds with stakeholder engagement and detailed feasibility work.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME RESTRUCTURES LEADERSHIP TO ACCELERATE GROWTH IN SHIPPING AND ENERGY

Crowley Maritime announced on January 6, 2026 a strategic consolidation of its business structure, creating two divisions: Shipping and Logistics led by Executive Vice President and Division President James C. Fowler, and Energy led by Executive Vice President and Division President Kollin Fencil. The move is intended to boost efficiency, deliver greater value to customers and position the company for accelerated growth across its shipping, logistics and energy activities.The Shipping and Logistics Division brings together Crowley's logistics, land transportation and global ship management operations to serve commercial and government customers with ocean shipping, inland trucking, multimodal transportation and warehousing. Fowler said the new structure will enable cohesive management of the entire transportation value chain and drive growth through safe, efficient supply-chain solutions.The Energy Division consolidates Crowley Fuels in Alaska, LNG services and advanced energy solutions. Fencil noted the reorganization will enable Crowley to innovate and scale more effectively in traditional and emerging energy markets, reinforcing the company's long-standing support of diverse energy needs across the United States. Since joining Crowley as senior vice presidents in 2023, Fowler and Fencil have helped improve financial performance and operational efficiency. Chief Operating Officer Megan Davidson said the realignment will accelerate decision-making and improve the company's ability to respond to market demands, supporting Crowley's position as a leader in maritime, energy and logistics.

+ CROWLEY AND SEACOR FORGE FAIRWATER HOLDINGS TO UNITE JONES ACT TANKER FLEET

Crowley Maritime and SEACOR Holdings, via its Seabulk Tankers unit, have formed a joint venture named Fairwater Holdings to integrate their liquid energy and chemical transport assets and to operate as an independent U.S. Jones Act service provider. The agreement was finalized on January 2, 2026.Fairwater will combine the strengths of both companies to offer a range of transportation solutions across the petroleum and chemical trades, along with third-party ship management services. The venture will oversee a fleet of 20 ocean-going articulated tug-barges and 11 tankers, largely under long-term charters, and will also provide crewing and technical management for 21 additional vessels owned by third parties.Daniel Thorogood, currently CEO of Seabulk, will become Fairwater's CEO after the deal closes. Tom Crowley, chairman and CEO of Crowley Maritime, said the partnership aligns with Crowley's growth strategy and will strengthen its leadership in the U.S. maritime market. Operations are expected to begin after customary closing conditions and regulatory approvals, with Fairwater set to commence in the first quarter of 2024.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ CMA CGM LOGISTICS [3 News That Matters]

+ MAWANI EXPANDS MARITIME CONNECTIVITY WITH CMA CGM'S OCEAN RISE SERVICE

RIYADH: Maritime connectivity between Saudi Arabia and key Asian and European markets is expanding after Mawani added CMACGM's Ocean Rise service to Jeddah Islamic Port. The service began on 3rd of May 2026 and connects Jeddah with Kobe, Nagoya, and Yokohama in Japan; Xiamen, Yantian, and Nansha in China; Rotterdam in the Netherlands; Hamburg in Germany; and Southampton in the UK.Mawani said the service has a handling capacity of up to 3,001 TEUs and aims to support Saudi exports and imports in cooperation with international shipping lines. The move aligns with the National Transport and Logistics Strategy to reinforce the Kingdom's standing as a global logistics hub connecting three continents.Mawani noted that this period has seen the launch of 18 maritime shipping services with a combined capacity of 123,552 TEUs, aimed at ensuring efficient delivery of goods and strengthening readiness across Saudi ports in collaboration with major carriers including MSC, Maersk, PIL, and Hapag-Lloyd. The Kingdom has also seen port activity grow, with March cargo throughput rising 6.66 percent year on year and 667,882 TEUs handled in February, up 20.89 percent from a year earlier. Jeddah Islamic Port remains a key gateway with advanced facilities to boost international trade.

+ CMA CGM EXPANDS INDIAN REGISTRY WITH TWO ADDITIONAL VESSELS

CMACGM is transferring two more containerships into the Indian registry, bringing its Indian-flag fleet to six. The moves add to earlier transfers of CMA CGM Victoria, CMA CGM Mendelssohn, CMA CGM Diamond and CMA CGM Manaus, and come as the group responds to new cabotage rules in India.The Vila do Conde, a 33,434-dwt vessel built in 2009 with a capacity of 2,556 TEU, was moved from the Maltese flag to India and has been part of CMACGM's fleet since 2023. Papers have also been filed for the transfer of the Semarang, built in 2007 with 38,000 DWT and a 2,700 TEU capacity.The moves underscore CMACGM's strategy to align with India's tightened cabotage regime, which bars foreign-flagged ships from carrying domestic cargo. Since January 2026, the Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways has rolled back an exemption that allowed some transshipment by foreign vessels. CMA CGM has already begun employing Indian seafarers on its registered ships, with around 1,000 currently and a target of 1,500 by year-end. The company also signed a February 2026 contract with Cochin Shipyard to build LNG-powered new vessels to support growth and sustainability in the region.

+ CMA CGM ADVANCES AI-FIRST STRATEGY TO REDEFINE INDIA'S LOGISTICS LANDSCAPE

As supply chains grow more volatile and customer expectations more exacting, logistics is undergoing a profound reinvention. CMA CGM Group explains how India is becoming a strategic growth market and a global innovation hub, with AI, ecosystem partnerships, and digital platforms reshaping everything from port operations to customer service. The result is a shift from reactive logistics to predictive, intelligent, and increasingly autonomous supply chains.On 28th April 2026, CMA CGM highlighted its commitment to artificial intelligence to boost operational efficiencies in the logistics sector. It is pursuing AI-driven solutions to optimize supply chain management and reduce disruptions from volatile markets, using advanced analytics and machine learning to build a more resilient network that meets evolving customer demands.CMACGM's strategy centers on alliances with Indian tech firms to create an ecosystem that supports AI integration into logistics, while also investing in digital platforms that improve transparency and real-time shipment tracking. The company aims to embed AI as a core operational element, enabling smarter decision-making, better service, and stronger growth for e-commerce and manufacturing.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

Crowley Maritime Corporation, founded in 1892, is a family-owned logistics and maritime services company headquartered in Jacksonville, Florida. + CROWLEY MARITIME [3 News That Matters]

+ CROWLEY MARITIME ADVANCES ALASKA GROWTH WITH PORT MCKENZIE FUEL TERMINAL FEASIBILITY

If developed, the project could streamline Crowley's operations and help catalyze additional investment in the area.With the permit in place, Crowley moves forward with feasibility assessments and engagement with local stakeholders, marking a potential milestone for the company and for Port McKenzie as they explore future growth opportunities.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME ANNOUNCES DUAL-DIVISION REALIGNMENT TO ENHANCE OPERATIONAL AGILITY

Crowley Maritime announced on January 6, 2026 the formation of two new divisions—Shipping and Logistics led by James C. Fowler, and Energy led by Kollin Fencil—as part of a strategic consolidation to improve execution and accelerate growth across its shipping, logistics and energy activities.The Shipping and Logistics Division combines Crowley's logistics, land transportation and global ship management to serve commercial and government customers with ocean shipping, inland trucking, multimodal transportation and warehousing.Fowler said the reorganized structure will enable cohesive management of the entire transportation value chain, driving growth through safe and efficient supply-chain solutions.The Energy Division consolidates Crowley Fuels in Alaska, LNG services and advanced energy solutions, enabling greater innovation and scale in both traditional and emerging energy markets.Fencil noted the realignment will help Crowley meet evolving customer demands for traditional fuels and advanced energy solutions, and the leaders have delivered improvements in financial performance and operational efficiency since joining the company in 2023. Chief Operating Officer Megan Davidson said the realignment will speed decision-making and improve the company's ability to respond to market dynamics, reinforcing Crowley's status as a leader in maritime, energy and logistics. Crowley is privately held and U.S.-owned, with a footprint spanning 36 nations and territories and more than 130 years in business.

+ FAIRWATER HOLDINGS EMERGES AS NEW JONES ACT SERVICE PROVIDER FROM CROWLEY-SEACOR ALLIANCE

+ CMA CGM LOGISTICS [3 News That Matters]

+ SAUDI PORTS AUTHORITY LAUNCHES CMA CGM ROUTE TO STRENGTHEN TRADE LINKS

+ CMA CGM TRANSFERS TWO MORE SHIPS TO INDIA, LIFTS INDIAN-FLAG FLEET TO SIX

CMACGM has moved two more containerships into the Indian registry, expanding its Indian-flag fleet to six as the group solidifies its presence in the Indian market. The development follows earlier transfers of CMA CGM Victoria, CMA CGM Mendelssohn, CMA CGM Diamond and CMA CGM Manaus.The Vila do Conde, a 33,434-dwt vessel built in 2009 with a capacity of 2,556 TEU, was re-flagged from Malta to India and has been part of CMACGM's fleet since 2023. + INDIA EMERGES AS GROWTH ENGINE AS CMA CGM PUSHES AI AND ECOSYSTEM PARTNERSHIPS IN LOGISTICS

The logistics sector is undergoing a transformation as supply chains become more volatile and customer expectations rise. The company is building AI-powered solutions to optimize supply chain management and curb disruptions caused by unpredictable market dynamics, leveraging analytics and machine learning to create a more resilient, responsive network for customers.CMACGM's plan involves forging partnerships with Indian tech companies to build a robust ecosystem that enables AI integration across logistics operations. This collaborative model not only accelerates innovation but also positions India as a pivotal player in the global logistics landscape. The firm envisions AI as a core element of its operating framework, driving smarter decision-making and enhanced customer service.The company is also investing in digital platforms that boost transparency and real-time shipment tracking. This capability helps businesses make informed decisions and improves overall performance, with the aim of delivering a seamless client experience and accelerating growth in e-commerce and manufacturing.With CMACGM leading the charge, the industry stands on the cusp of significant change. Its AI-first strategy highlights how ongoing innovation is essential to maintaining a competitive edge, and CMACGM's India-focused initiatives could serve as a model for others navigating modern, complex supply chains.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

value.Title: YALE UNIVERSITY - THE RECENT NEWS IN THE SECTOR AND ITS PEERS

urn:contentItem:6JJF-N343-RTCX-44YS-00000-00YALE UNIVERSITY - THE RECENT NEWS IN THE SECTOR AND ITS PEERS2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:40:41ZLexisNexisSTOCKYALE UNIVERSITY - THE RECENT NEWS IN THE SECTOR AND ITS PEERS

DASHBOARD: YALE UNIVERSITY

Websitehttps://www.yale.edu/IndustryHigher EducationAddressNew Haven, Connecticut 06520

ACTIVITIES:

Yale University, founded in 1701, is an Ivy League institution in New Haven, Connecticut. With around 15,000 employees and 12,000 students, it generates approximately $4.5 billion in annual revenue. Yale excels in humanities, sciences, and professional schools like law and medicine, with notable alumni including five U.S. presidents. In 2024, it expanded sustainability research. Competing with Harvard and Princeton, Yales mission is to educate leaders and advance discovery through rigorous scholarship and global outreach.

+ YALE UNIVERSITY [3 News That Matters]

+ YALE UNIVERSITY REVEALS UPDATED CLIMATE OPINION MAPS FOR 2025

Yale University has released its updated Climate Opinion Maps, providing a detailed view of U.S. public sentiment regarding climate change and energy policy. This latest version, published on the 5th of May 2026, reveals a significant shift in attitudes toward renewable energy sources across the nation, particularly in support for solar and wind energy projects.The updated Yale Climate Opinion Maps introduce four critical measures: support for local solar farms, local wind farms, opposition to fossil fuel drilling on public land, and preference for candidates who advocate for climate action. Notably, over half of the states in the U.S. show majority support for solar (44 states) and wind farms (29 states), indicating a clear trend towards renewable energy solutions in local communities.Further analysis from Yale University indicates that a substantial 57% of Americans are inclined to vote for candidates who prioritize action on global warming. However, this preference varies significantly across different regions, from as low as 40% in West Virginia to as high as 87% in the District of Columbia. This local disparity highlights the complex relationship between geographic location and climate policy support.Despite federal efforts to limit renewable energy projects, the momentum for solar and wind energy continues to grow, with notable increases in solar generation (up 37%) and wind energy (up 12%) from the previous year. States like California, Texas, and Florida lead the way in public support for local solar initiatives, reinforcing the trend of local advocacy for clean energy solutions.The comprehensive data provided by the Yale Climate Opinion Maps serves as a vital resource for understanding public attitudes toward climate change and energy policies. As the country navigates a pivotal moment in addressing climate issues, Yale University's findings will be instrumental for policymakers, educators, and stakeholders aiming to align with the evolving public sentiment around renewable energy.

+ SOPHIE BUCHOWSKI JOINS YALE UNIVERSITY AS A PROMISING SCHOLAR ATHLETE

Sophie Buchowski, a standout athlete from John Burroughs School, has earned the title of the 2026 Post-Dispatch Scholar Athlete. This recognition highlights her exceptional performance both in academics and athletics, particularly in field hockey and lacrosse. Buchowski, who has a unique hobby of creating her own version of slime, will be taking her talents to Yale University, where she plans to continue her field hockey career while pursuing a degree in medicine.On the 5th of May, 2026, it was announced that Buchowski would join Yale University, a prestigious institution known for its rigorous academic programs and strong athletic programs. The Scholar Athlete initiative acknowledges students across various high schools who demonstrate excellence in sports as well as in the classroom. Buchowski's achievements in athletics and her commitment to academic excellence make her a fitting representative of this program.Buchowski's journey is not only marked by her athletic prowess but also by her academic aspirations. As she prepares to transition to Yale University, she aims to balance her demanding schedule of sports and studies. Her dedication to both her athletic and academic pursuits reflects a growing trend among student-athletes who aspire to excel in multiple domains. This achievement is a testament to her hard work and a source of inspiration for her peers.The Yale University athletic department has expressed excitement over Buchowski's commitment, emphasizing the value of student-athletes in fostering a culture of excellence. With a focus on developing athletes who are also scholars, Yale encourages its students to pursue their passions and goals, both on and off the field. Buchowski's dual commitment to sports and medicine aligns perfectly with this mission.As Buchowski embarks on this new chapter at Yale University, she joins a roster of talented student-athletes who have similarly balanced their academic and athletic careers. Her story serves as a reminder of the opportunities available to those who are dedicated and driven. The future looks bright for Sophie Buchowski as she steps onto the field at Yale, eager to make her mark both as an athlete and a future medical professional.

+ YALE UNIVERSITY UNVEILS NEW MISSION STATEMENT AMID TRUST CONCERNS

Yale University has introduced a new mission statement aimed at restoring public trust in higher education. The revised statement, which reads: 'Yale’s core mission is to create, disseminate, and preserve knowledge through research and teaching,' comes in the wake of a university-commissioned report released on 15th April 2026 that outlined key recommendations for enhancing the institution's credibility.The change marks a significant departure from Yale's previous mission statement, which was more expansive and included phrases such as 'improving the world today' and 'educating aspiring leaders worldwide.' Yale's new statement is notably concise, reflecting the report's emphasis on clarity and purpose in higher education. The committee of ten professors responsible for the report highlighted 'widespread uncertainty about the fundamental purpose and mission of higher education,' urging a more focused approach.Yale's President, Maurie McInnis, who initiated the committee's formation last year, acknowledged the report as an 'honest assessment' of the university's role in the decline of public trust. In her end-of-year message to the community, she indicated that the university had already begun implementing many of the committee's recommendations, including the adoption of this new mission statement.The report specifically criticized the previous mission statement's complexity, arguing that while its goals were commendable, they detracted from the core purpose of a university. The committee asserted that without a clear mission, it becomes challenging to measure whether educational institutions are fulfilling their commitments. The new statement aligns closely with a model referenced in the report, which aims to rebuild trust in Yale and similar institutions.In her message, McInnis emphasized the importance of public confidence in higher education, stating that the university's work must resonate with societal needs. She reaffirmed Yale's commitment to addressing global challenges and enhancing educational accessibility, including initiatives like free tuition for more students. As Yale continues to navigate the evolving landscape of higher education, McInnis plans to provide further updates in the coming months, reinforcing the institution's mission and priorities.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ BROWN UNIVERSITY [3 News That Matters]

+ BROWN UNIVERSITY INTRODUCES ONLINE MASTER'S IN PUBLIC POLICY

PROVIDENCE, R.I. — As communities across the world face increasingly complex public policy challenges, Brown University has launched a new master’s degree program to equip students with the frameworks, global perspectives, and leadership skills needed to inform effective policy.Announced on the 4th of May, 2026, this 16-month online master of public policy is designed specifically for working professionals, who will be encouraged to bring real-world challenges into the classroom. “Brown’s master of public policy is a virtual incubator for tomorrow’s policy leaders,” said David Blanding, an associate professor of the practice of international and public affairs and director of the new master’s program. “The program empowers experienced professionals to fortify their expertise through an innovative curriculum, using global insights from world-class faculty to positively impact their workplaces — and the world.”The program draws on Brown’s signature student-centered learning approach and focuses on four primary learning areas: understanding policy ecosystems, analyzing policy using data, applying frameworks across various fields of study such as history, sociology, and political science, and developing leadership skills in areas including strategic communication and stakeholder engagement. “Leadership isn’t taught in a single course — it’s threaded across the entire program,” Blanding noted.This initiative is part of Brown University’s growing portfolio of online master’s degrees, aimed at making its world-class academics more accessible to learners globally. The master of public policy is tailored for professionals already working in policy and related roles across government, nonprofit, and private organizations who are looking to deepen their expertise and advance into senior leadership positions.The first cohort for this innovative online program is set to begin in January 2027, and program leaders are currently accepting applications, marking a significant step in Brown University’s commitment to fostering the next generation of public policy leaders.

+ YO-YO MA REFLECTS ON MUSIC'S ROLE AT BROWN UNIVERSITY EVENT

PROVIDENCE, R.I. — After renowned cellist Yo-Yo Ma sat down on stage in the De Ciccio Family Auditorium in Brown’s Salomon Center for Teaching, he immediately picked up his bow. In a somber start to his speaking engagement, Ma played a Bach cello suite for a few minutes as a way to recognize the tragic shooting that took place at Brown on 13th December 2025.Following the short musical performance, Ma — who was born in Paris to Chinese parents and has lived in Massachusetts since the 1970s — engaged in a wide-ranging discussion moderated by Brown University Associate Professor of Music Anthony Cheung. The event was organized by the student-led Brown China Summit and drew attention to the intersection of music, education, and identity.In his talk, Ma emphasized the profound impact of a liberal arts education. He stated, "Everything I’ve done since college — every project I’ve done, every time I think about anything — the root of inspiration came from something that happened in college." This sentiment resonated with students as he encouraged them to explore their curiosities beyond their majors, highlighting subjects such as anthropology and evolutionary biology as keys to understanding humanity.Ma also delved into his cultural heritage, explaining how he encompasses various aspects of his identity rather than choosing one over another. He described himself as a human being interested in understanding his roots, stating, "I was interested [in college] about what created China in the first place." His reflections on identity were a reminder of the importance of embracing diverse backgrounds in a cohesive manner.The renowned cellist concluded his talk by addressing the evolving nature of music in the context of artificial intelligence. He posited that the motivation behind musical creation remains inherently human, prompting listeners to consider the emotional and psychological elements that contribute to music. Ma’s insights into the significance of music as both a scientific and artistic endeavor captivated the audience, leaving them with a deeper appreciation for the connection between humanity and sound.

+ BROWN UNIVERSITY TRACKS RISING FUEL COSTS AMID IRAN WAR IMPACT

Unleaded gas prices have surged to $4.09 per gallon at the Marathon station on Point Street in Providence, reflecting a broader trend tracked by researchers at Brown University. As of the 5th of May 2026, Rhode Island residents have collectively spent an additional $36.6 million at the gas pump since the onset of the U.S.-Israeli conflict in Iran at the end of February.The Iran War Energy Cost Tracker, an innovative online tool developed by the Climate Solutions Lab at Brown University’s Watson School of International and Public Affairs, provides a comprehensive analysis of gasoline and diesel prices across the nation. Continuously updated with data from AAA, the U.S. Energy Information Administration, and the U.S. Census Bureau, this dashboard estimates the financial impact of rising fuel prices against a pre-war baseline.According to the tracker, the total consumer burden due to increased fuel costs has reached an alarming $29 billion nationwide since February 28. This figure surpasses the total budget of NASA for the federal fiscal year 2026 by over $5 billion, as highlighted by Jeff Colgan, a political science professor and head of the Climate Solutions Lab at Brown University. Colgan remarked, "We could be doing great things with this money as a country that we’re not. We’re instead spending it on extra fuel costs imposed by a war."In Rhode Island, the average price for a gallon of gas stands at $4.18, marking a $1.29 increase from before the conflict began. Households in the state have seen an average rise of $83.78 in their gas expenditures since the war commenced. Comparatively, while Rhode Island's prices are higher than before, they remain somewhat lower than those in other New England states, with Connecticut facing the highest average at $4.33 per gallon.As fuel prices continue to escalate, Rhode Island Governor Dan McKee is leveraging this situation to advocate for a rollback of the state’s 2-cent gas tax increase implemented last July. His proposal aims to reduce the gas tax from 40 cents to 38 cents per gallon, which he estimates would save Rhode Islanders approximately $9 million annually. However, Colgan argues that such savings are minimal compared to the broader economic implications of the ongoing conflict, urging for a shift towards sustainable energy solutions that are less vulnerable to geopolitical tensions.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ PRINCETON UNIVERSITY [3 News That Matters]

+ PRINCETON'S KAMERON MERCER RECEIVES OFFER FROM CINCINNATI BEARCATS

Princeton High School's five-star basketball prospect Kameron Mercer has officially received a scholarship offer from the University of Cincinnati Bearcats. On 6th May 2026, Mercer announced the offer on his social media accounts, marking a significant milestone in his burgeoning athletic career.Mercer played a pivotal role in leading his Princeton High School team to victory in the Ohio Division I state basketball title as a sophomore. The 6-foot-5 shooting guard showcased his potential throughout the season, culminating in a 25-3 record for the Vikings. He averaged 14.2 points, 5.4 rebounds, and 3.4 assists per game, with notable performances including a season-high of 27 points against Colerain in January.The Cincinnati Bearcats are not alone in their pursuit of Mercer; they join an impressive list of programs including Florida State, Maryland, and Ohio State that have shown interest in the talented guard. Originally offered by the Bearcats in October 2024 while he was at Huntington Expressions Prep in West Virginia, Mercer’s recent visit to Cincinnati led to a renewed offer under new head coach Jerrod Calhoun.Mercer's impressive stats from the past season include being third in the Greater Miami Conference for assists and second in steals. His ability to perform against older competition at Huntington, averaging 11 points, six rebounds, and five assists, has only further elevated his profile among college recruiters. Additionally, he achieved two double-doubles during the season, demonstrating his all-around game.Accompanying Mercer on his visit to the Cincinnati Bearcats was his older brother, Jaylen Mercer, who is also garnering attention from various college programs as a promising edge rusher. Jaylen, a 6-foot-5, 260-pound athlete, has received offers from multiple schools, including the Bearcats, as he prepares for his graduation in 2027. This family connection is likely to play a role in Kameron's decision as he weighs the opportunities presented by the Bearcats and other schools.

+ PRINCETON'S YIYUN LI SECURES PULITZER PRIZE FOR MEMOIR

Princeton University professor Yiyun Li has been awarded the 2026 Pulitzer Prize for memoir for her impactful book 'Things in Nature Merely Grow.' The announcement came as a pleasant surprise to Li, who received a flurry of congratulatory messages on the afternoon of 5th May 2026.The Pulitzer Prize for memoir honors distinguished factual accounts by American authors. Li's book is described as 'a writer’s deeply moving and revelatory account of losing her younger son to suicide a little more than six years after her older son died in the same manner.' This method allowed them to capture a 'molecular movie' of polaritons in motion, revealing their long-range propagation capabilities. The findings indicate that MoOCl2 could serve as a promising platform for next-generation optoelectronic devices, including computer chips that leverage light instead of electricity.As the UChicago researchers continue to explore this novel material, they aim to uncover fundamental questions regarding the interaction of light with the crystal's atomic structure. However, the program's status has fluctuated dramatically over the years. As of 5th May 2026, the landscape of NCAA Division I hockey has shifted, yet the University of Pennsylvania remains a notable case study in the viability of collegiate hockey programs.The university's hockey program was once a proud Division I Independent, established in 1965, and became a member of the Eastern College Athletic Conference (ECAC) shortly thereafter. During its early years, the program enjoyed significant support, highlighted by alumni from the Class of 1923 who funded the construction of the Class of 1923 Arena, a facility that still stands today as a potential venue for NCAA hockey.Despite this promising start, the program faced an abrupt termination in 1978, leading to widespread discontent among students and alumni. The decision to discontinue the hockey team, part of broader budget cuts, sparked protests, including a sit-in at College Hall, where administrative offices are located. While other programs were reinstated, the hockey team was not, leaving a gap that has not been filled since. Currently, the university fields a club team that competes in ACHA Division II, utilizing the historic Class of 1923 Arena as its home.The local hockey market has been invigorated by the Philadelphia Flyers, an NHL franchise that has captivated the region since their inception in 1967. The Flyers' success and storied history, including two Stanley Cup championships, underscore the potential for collegiate hockey in Philadelphia. As the Flyers advance in the 2026 Stanley Cup Playoffs, the interest in hockey remains palpable, raising questions about the future of the University of Pennsylvania's program.Despite an endowment announced in July 2019 that aimed to restore Division I hockey by the 2025-26 NCAA season, there have been few updates or indications that this goal will be achieved. Former Head Coach Bob Finke poignantly remarked on the treatment of players during the program’s dissolution, emphasizing the emotional impact on young athletes. The narrative surrounding men's hockey at the University of Pennsylvania continues to evolve, and further insights can be found in 'The Story of Men's Hockey at Penn,' a detailed piece by The Daily Pennsylvanian, which promises to delve deeper into this significant aspect of the university's athletic history.

+ UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA ALUMNUS JOHN TERNUS TO LEAD APPLE AS NEW CEO

Apple has officially announced that John Ternus, a distinguished alumnus of the University of Pennsylvania, will assume the role of CEO starting September 1. This significant leadership transition was communicated on 29th April 2026, marking a new chapter for the tech giant.Ternus will succeed Tim Cook, who will transition to the role of executive chairman. In a statement, Cook expressed his confidence in Ternus, declaring him 'without question the right person to lead Apple into the future.' Cook will remain in his current position until the transition is complete, collaborating closely with Ternus during this period.Joining Apple in 2001, Ternus has amassed 25 years of experience within the company, currently serving as the senior vice president of hardware engineering. His leadership has been instrumental in the development of iconic products such as the iPhone, iPad, AirPods, and Apple Watch, showcasing his deep understanding of the company’s core offerings.A proud graduate of the University of Pennsylvania, Ternus earned his bachelor's degree in mechanical engineering in 1997 and was an active member of the men’s swim team. His academic background and extensive experience at Apple position him as a strong leader capable of guiding the company amid increasing competition from rivals like Microsoft and Google, particularly in the realm of generative AI.Upon taking the reins as CEO, Ternus will also join Apple’s board, reinforcing his pivotal role in shaping the company's strategic direction. As Apple navigates the evolving technology landscape, the leadership change is seen as a crucial step in ensuring the company's continued innovation and market leadership.

+ PENN WELCOMES APOLLO ASSET MANAGEMENT CO-PRESIDENT FOR INSIGHTS ON FINANCE CAREERS

The University of Pennsylvania hosted Scott Kleinman, co-president of Apollo Asset Management, Inc. and founder of the Kleinman Center for Energy Policy, for an engaging discussion on finance careers. The event took place on 23rd April 2026 at Steinberg-Dietrich Hall, where Kleinman shared insights from his extensive career and perspectives on the evolving finance landscape.Moderated by Muhammad Vakil, the president of the Wharton Undergraduate Private Equity & Venture Capital Club, the fireside chat allowed Kleinman to emphasize the critical role students play as future alumni of Penn and Wharton. He expressed that their contributions can significantly shape the business environment and address societal challenges. Vakil highlighted the relevance of Kleinman's views on the intersection of energy policy and geopolitics, as well as the leadership necessary in private markets.During the discussion, Kleinman articulated the importance of mentorship in navigating the complexities of the finance sector, particularly in light of advancements in artificial intelligence. He advocated for students to become domain experts, noting that while AI can enhance efficiency, it cannot replace the value of human expertise. 'Finding ways to marry the two is where I’ve seen that superpower come to life,' Kleinman remarked, underscoring the necessity of combining technology with deep industry knowledge.Kleinman’s journey with Apollo Asset Management began in 1996 when he joined as the company's 13th employee. He recounted how Apollo transformed from a niche private equity firm into a global platform, credited largely to its contrarian approach and the dedication of its employees. The co-president reflected on his foundational experiences, including his initial role as an investment banking analyst, which instilled in him a relentless intellectual curiosity and a robust understanding of market dynamics.The event concluded with an interactive Q&A session, where students expressed their admiration for Kleinman and his contributions to both the finance sector and the Penn community. His insights into the challenges and opportunities within private markets resonated with many attendees, who were eager to learn about the future of energy and finance. As the discussion wrapped up, Kleinman left the audience with a call to action: to leverage their education and experiences to drive meaningful change in their respective fields.

+ STANFORD UNIVERSITY [3 News That Matters]

+ STANFORD UNIVERSITY UNCOVERS DISTINCT GENETIC LINEAGE OF 'GHOST ELEPHANTS'

In a groundbreaking discovery, researchers at Stanford University have revealed that a population of elephants in Angola's high-altitude wetlands possesses a unique genetic lineage. This finding was made possible through the extraction of DNA from dung, leading to the identification of these so-called 'ghost elephants'. The study was formally documented on 6th May 2026, and it has drawn considerable attention from the conservation community and the general public alike.Conservation biologist Steve Boyes has long sought to uncover the truth behind these elusive creatures, which are believed to be nocturnal giants residing in a remote part of Angola. After capturing their image with a motion-sensor camera in 2024, Boyes partnered with scientists from Stanford University to delve deeper into the genetic origins of these elephants. The results were unexpected; the ghost elephants are genetically distinct from any previously sequenced populations and share a closer resemblance to elephants found in Namibia, located hundreds of miles to the south.Led by Dmitri Petrov, the Michelle and Kevin Douglas Professor at Stanford, the genomic analysis utilized advanced techniques to decode the genetic material found in fecal samples. Petrov emphasized the growing capabilities of DNA sequencing technologies. 'DNA is the molecule of life, and people have figured out how to read it faster and faster,' he stated, highlighting the potential of such research to inform conservation efforts.The quest to understand the ghost elephants is part of a broader narrative captured in a National Geographic documentary directed by Werner Herzog. The film chronicles Boyes' journey through the rugged terrain of Angola, showcasing the challenges faced by the research team. The collaboration between Boyes and Stanford scientists, including senior scientist Katie Solari, has not only provided insights into the genetics of these elephants but also raised new questions about their evolutionary history and ecological significance.As the research progresses, Stanford University scientists hope to uncover more about the ghost elephants' population dynamics and their relationship to other elephant populations. The study has already yielded crucial information about individual identification and genetic diversity, underscoring the importance of non-invasive methods in wildlife research. With ongoing investigations, the story of Angola's ghost elephants continues to unfold, offering a compelling glimpse into the complexities of conservation biology.

+ STANFORD UNIVERSITY STUDY REVEALS AI FEEDBACK BIAS FAVORING BLACK AND FEMALE STUDENTS

A recent study from Stanford University has found that artificial intelligence grading systems may provide more positive feedback to black and female students while offering more criticism to white students. On 6th May 2026, the study titled "Marked Pedagogies: Examining Linguistic Biases in Personalized Automated Writing Feedback" analyzed approximately 600 essays written by middle school students, focusing on how AI-generated feedback varied based on demographic descriptors assigned to each student.Researchers submitted each essay to an AI model 13 times, altering descriptors such as race, gender, motivation level, and learning ability. The findings revealed what researchers termed "positive feedback bias" and "feedback withholding bias," indicating a concerning disparity in the treatment of students based on their identities. The study highlighted that students identified as Black, Hispanic, Asian, female, unmotivated, and learning-disabled received less constructive criticism and more praise compared to their counterparts, while male, White, motivated, and high-achieving students faced more distant feedback.The feedback provided to black students was often identity-based, with responses such as, "Consider how community service can specifically empower you and your peers as young Black leaders." In contrast, feedback aimed at white students was more focused on content, including directions like, "Try to avoid informal language and first-person perspective in an argumentative essay." Such differences in feedback raise critical questions about the effectiveness of AI in educational settings.Mei Tan, the lead author of the study, expressed concerns regarding the implications of these findings. In an interview, Tan stated that the AI models appear to be reflecting the biases inherent in human behavior. "Maybe a takeaway is that we shouldn’t leave the pedagogy to the large language model," Tan emphasized, suggesting that human oversight remains essential in educational contexts where AI is increasingly utilized.As schools continue to adopt automated tools for grading and instruction, the results of the Stanford University study underscore the need for careful consideration of how AI systems are designed and implemented. The findings prompt a broader discussion about the role of artificial intelligence in education and the potential risks of perpetuating existing biases through automated feedback mechanisms.

+ STANFORD UNIVERSITY FELLOWSHIP AWAITS UH HILO'S HEALANI ROBACK

Healani Roback, a double major in linguistics and Japanese studies graduating from the University of Hawai i at Hilo on 16th May 2026, has been awarded a prestigious fellowship program at Stanford University in Japan. Roback's acceptance into the Toshizo Watanabe Fellowship Program is a testament to his dedication and proficiency in the Japanese language, which he attributes to his undergraduate study abroad experience in Japan.The Stanford University fellowship, part of the Inter-University Center for Japanese Language Studies (IUC), is designed for students aspiring to deepen their linguistic and cultural knowledge focused on Japan. According to the fellowship's webpage, it aims to cultivate future leaders in various fields. Roback expressed that his time studying abroad was pivotal in attaining the level of language proficiency required for the fellowship, which includes a $35,000 full-tuition scholarship for the 10-month program.Raised in Ewa Beach on O ahu and a graduate of James Campbell High School, Roback began his studies at UH Hilo in 2022. His experiences at Rikkyo University in Japan, supported by an Audrey S. Furukawa Scholarship, provided him with a broader perspective on the world. Roback noted that the density of Tokyo's population was both surprising and inspiring, prompting him to consider how he can contribute to global understanding through his work as a translator, although he has yet to finalize his plans for graduate school.In addition to the Stanford fellowship, Roback has been awarded nearly $5,000 from the Shoyu Club scholarship and a $5,000 IUC Alumni Fund scholarship. These financial supports have been instrumental in enabling him to pursue his ambitions in linguistics and translation. He expressed gratitude for the opportunities presented by his academic journey at the University of Hawai i at Hilo and continues to look ahead to his future endeavors.Roback's story reflects the potential of students from the University of Hawai i at Hilo to excel on international stages, particularly in fields that bridge cultures and languages. As he prepares for his upcoming fellowship at Stanford University, he emphasizes the importance of his study abroad experience in shaping his aspirations and enhancing his skills, making him a promising candidate for leadership in Japanese language studies.

urn:contentItem:6JJ2-1XJ3-S60R-W4RR-00000-00Walt Disney Company Accelerates Renewable Energy Push with New Solar Farm2026-05-09T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:40:40ZLexisNexisWalt Disney Company Accelerates Renewable Energy Push with New Solar Farm

AMERICAN DAILY STOCK REPORT

UNITED STATES EDITION

08 May 2026 11:17 EDT

The Walt Disney Company is making significant strides in its renewable energy initiatives with the addition of a new solar farm, which is set to fully meet the daytime energy needs of Walt Disney World Resort. Energized in late April, the 74.5 MW facility is located in Levy County, Florida, approximately 130 miles northwest of the resort. This development, announced on 6th May 2026, complements three existing solar arrays near Disney property in Lake Buena Vista, further solidifying the company's commitment to sustainable energy.

The latest installation spans about 484 acres and is operated by Bronson Solar and the Central Florida Tourism Oversight District. This facility allows Disney to diversify its solar energy procurement across multiple locations in the Sunshine State. Representatives from Disney Parks & Resorts stated, "On a sunny day in spring or summer, together four solar projects can produce up to 100% of the daytime power needs of the entire resort—covering four theme parks, two water parks, and numerous hotels."

The environmental benefits of this solar project are substantial. By transitioning to solar power, Disney estimates a reduction of over 140,000 metric tons in greenhouse gas emissions annually. This shift is equivalent to powering the company's monorail system for 34 years, underscoring the significance of the new facility in Disney's broader sustainability goals.

The Walt Disney Company has been actively pursuing renewable energy solutions, aiming for net-zero carbon emissions by 2030. This initiative is part of a larger strategy that includes solar installations at Disneyland Resort in California and various sites in Asia and Europe. Notably, Disneyland Paris boasts the largest solar canopy plant in Europe, producing enough energy to power a town of over 17,400 people. Additionally, Disney has made strides in solar energy for its cruise operations in the Bahamas.

With a combined solar capacity of 212,000 kilowatts from four sites, Walt Disney World now utilizes more than 600,000 solar panels, generating sufficient energy to power over 19,000 Florida homes for a year. As Disney continues to innovate and expand its renewable energy footprint, the new solar farm stands as a testament to the company's commitment to sustainability and its vision for a cleaner, greener future.

SECTION 1 WALT DISNEY RECENT PRESS RELEASES

December 12, 2025: Walt Disney announces dividend

Walt Disney today announced an interim dividend of 75.0c per share. The ex-dividend date is Monday, December 15 and it is payable on Thursday, January 15, 2026.

SECTION 2 WALT DISNEY ACTIVITIES

Activities

The Walt Disney Company, a global entertainment conglomerate listed on the New York Stock Exchange, specializes in creating and distributing family-friendly content across multiple platforms. Its core business activities include media networks such as ABC and ESPN, film and television production through its studios, theme parks and resorts, consumer products, and interactive media. The company leverages its iconic brands, including Marvel, Pixar, and Star Wars, to engage audiences worldwide. In recent years, the fastest-growing segments have been direct-to-consumer streaming services like Disney+ and Hulu, which have expanded subscriber bases through original content and international markets, as well as the parks and experiences division, which has rebounded strongly with innovative attractions and record visitor numbers, driving overall revenue growth. It is the NYSE's largest Broadcasting & entertainment company by market capitalisation.
